fields: <svg width="100" height="40" viewBox="0 0 100 40"><path d="M 10 20 L 90 5 M 10 20 L 90 35" fill="none" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/></svg>
<svg width="100" height="40" viewBox="0 0 100 40"><path d="M 10 5 L 90 20 M 10 35 L 90 20" fill="none" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/></svg>
Staccato: Short and detached (dot above/below note)
<svg width="100" height="40" viewBox="0 0 100 40"><path d="M 10 30 Q 50 0 90 30" fill="none" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/></svg>
Tenuto: Held for its full value (line above/below note)
Treble Clef: G-clef, used for high pitches
Bass Clef: F-clef, used for low pitches
Alto Clef: C-clef centered on 3rd line
Tenor Clef: C-clef centered on 4th line
Treble and Bass staves joined by a brace
Two lines indicating a new section
Indicates the end of the piece
Tells beats per measure and beat value
A segment of time defined by beats
<svg width="60" height="20" viewBox="0 0 60 20"><path d="M 5 5 Q 30 20 55 5" fill="none" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/></svg>
Tie: Connects two notes of the same pitch
<svg width="100" height="40" viewBox="0 0 100 40"><path d="M 10 30 Q 50 0 90 30" fill="none" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/></svg>
Slur: Connects notes of different pitches
Trill: Rapid alternation between two notes
Mordent: Single alternation with note above
Inverted Mordent: Single alternation with note below
Turn: Note above, note, note below, note
Inverted Turn: Note below, note, note above, note
Leaning note (takes half the main note value)
Segno: The sign used with D.S.
Repeat Sign: Repeat the music between the dots
Play the first time through a repeat
Play the second time through a repeat
Octave below: Play an octave lower
<svg width="20" height="60" viewBox="0 0 20 60"><path d="M 10 0 C 0 5 20 10 10 15 C 0 20 20 25 10 30 C 0 35 20 40 10 45 C 0 50 20 55 10 60" fill="none" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/></svg>
Arpeggio: Notes of a chord played in succession
Fermata: Hold the note longer than indicated
Caesura: A pause or break in music
Lamb of God (often used in Requiem/Mass)
Notes that sound the same but have different names (e.g., C# and Db)
Belonging to the current scale or key
Using notes outside the current scale/key
Emphasizing the weak beats or off-beats
Shifting music to a different pitch level
Changing from one key to another
Major and minor keys that share the same key signature
Major and minor keys that share the same tonic
A continually repeated musical phrase or rhythm
Restatement of a motif at a higher or lower pitch
Turning a melody or chord upside down
A melodic or harmonic configuration that creates a sense of resolution
Ending a minor-key piece with a major chord
A short musical idea or fragment
A complete musical thought used as a building block
The relationship between independent melodic lines
A contrapuntal composition based on a main theme (subject)
A collection of short musical pieces
An introductory piece to an opera or larger work
A large-scale musical work for orchestra and voices, usually on a religious theme
A self-contained piece for a single voice, usually in an opera
A style of delivery in which a singer is allowed to adopt the rhythms of ordinary speech
The quality or 'color' of a sound
How melodic, rhythmic, and harmonic materials are combined
A single melodic line without accompaniment
A primary melody with chordal accompaniment
Multiple versions of the same melody played simultaneously
Absence of a tonal center or key
Use of two different keys simultaneously
Use of intervals smaller than a semitone
A scale consisting entirely of whole steps
A scale consisting entirely of half steps
An eight-note scale alternating whole and half steps
Sharp: Raises a note by a half step
Flat: Lowers a note by a half step
Natural: Cancels a previous sharp or flat
Double Sharp: Raises a note by a whole step
Double Flat: Lowers a note by a whole step
An interval of 2 semitones (e.g., C to D)
An interval of 1 semitone (e.g., C to Db)
An interval of 4 semitones (e.g., C to E)
An interval of 3 semitones (e.g., C to Eb)
An interval of 5 semitones (e.g., C to F)
An interval of 7 semitones (e.g., C to G)
An interval of 9 semitones (e.g., C to A)
An interval of 8 semitones (e.g., C to Ab)
An interval of 11 semitones (e.g., C to B)
An interval of 10 semitones (e.g., C to Bb)
An interval of 12 semitones (e.g., C to C)
